d4e1_a.speaker: Mentor
d4e1_a.text: Day 4! Your business is getting noticed. A local influencer walks in and offers to promote your shop online in exchange for free products. How do you handle it?

d4e1_b.speaker: Influencer
d4e1_b.text: Hey! I have about 50,000 followers and I love your shop. I could feature it if you give me some free products. What do you say?

choices.text: Agree immediately — free exposure sounds great
Negotiate — offer a discount instead of free products
Ask to see their engagement stats first
Decline politely — you prefer organic growth

d4e1_c_agree.speaker: Influencer
d4e1_c_agree.text: Amazing! I will post about this today. Your shop is going to blow up online trust me!

d4e1_c_negotiate.speaker: Influencer
d4e1_c_negotiate.text: A discount instead? Hmm, fair enough. I respect the business mindset. Deal!

d4e1_c_verify.speaker: Influencer
d4e1_c_verify.text: Sure! Here are my analytics. Most followers are local and highly engaged. Pretty solid numbers right?

d4e1_c_decline.speaker: Influencer
d4e1_c_decline.text: Oh okay, no worries. Your loss I guess. Good luck with the shop though!

d4e1_result_agree.speaker: Mentor
d4e1_result_agree.text: Influencer marketing can be powerful but always set clear expectations upfront. A verbal agreement is not enough.

d4e1_result_negotiate.speaker: Mentor
d4e1_result_negotiate.text: Smart negotiation! Never give away full value when a partial deal serves both sides. Business is about mutual benefit.

d4e1_result_verify.speaker: Mentor
d4e1_result_verify.text: Due diligence before any deal is essential. Follower count means nothing without real engagement from the right audience.

d4e1_result_decline.speaker: Mentor
d4e1_result_decline.text: Organic growth is valid but do not dismiss partnerships outright. Evaluate each opportunity on its own merits.

d4e2_a.speaker: Mentor
d4e2_a.text: Midday. You discover one of your staff members has been giving unauthorized discounts to their friends. Sales are slightly lower because of it. What do you do?

d4e2_b.speaker: You
d4e2_b.text: This is a trust issue. How I handle this will set the tone for how my team operates...

choices.text: Have a private calm conversation and set clear boundaries
Dismiss them immediately for breach of trust

d4e2_c_talk.speaker: Staff
d4e2_c_talk.text: I am really sorry. I did not think it was a big deal but I understand now. It will not happen again.

d4e2_c_warning.speaker: Staff
d4e2_c_warning.text: A written warning? I understand. I will make sure this never happens again.

d4e2_c_dismiss.speaker: Other Staff
d4e2_c_dismiss.text: Wow they got fired just like that? I had no idea that was such a serious offense. Everyone is a bit shaken.

d4e2_c_evidence.speaker: You
d4e2_c_evidence.text: I need to be sure before I act. Let me review the receipts and security footage carefully.

d4e2_result_talk.speaker: Mentor
d4e2_result_talk.text: Direct honest communication resolves most workplace issues. People often make mistakes without realizing the full impact.

d4e2_result_warning.speaker: Mentor
d4e2_result_warning.text: Formal warnings create paper trails and signal seriousness. Good for repeat offenses but may feel heavy handed for a first incident.

d4e2_result_dismiss.speaker: Mentor
d4e2_result_dismiss.text: Immediate dismissal sends a strong message but can damage team morale. Reserve it for severe or repeated violations.

d4e2_result_evidence.speaker: Mentor
d4e2_result_evidence.text: Never act on suspicion alone. Gathering evidence before confronting protects both you and your employee from unfair outcomes.

d4e3_a.speaker: Mentor
d4e3_a.text: Late afternoon. A corporate buyer approaches you with a bulk purchase order worth a lot of money but they want a 30% discount and faster delivery than you can normally manage. What do you do?

d4e3_b.speaker: Corporate Buyer
d4e3_b.text: We want 200 units by Friday at a 30% discount. This could be a recurring order if you deliver. Can you make it happen?

choices.text: Accept the deal — big order is big revenue
Accept the discount but push the delivery to next week
Decline — you cannot compromise quality for speed

d4e3_c_accept.speaker: Corporate Buyer
d4e3_c_accept.text: Excellent! We will send the contract over. Make sure you deliver on time or the deal is off.

d4e3_c_negotiate.speaker: Corporate Buyer
d4e3_c_negotiate.text: Fifteen percent? You drive a hard bargain. But I respect that. Let us meet at 20% and call it a deal.

d4e3_c_delay.speaker: Corporate Buyer
d4e3_c_delay.text: Next week works actually. Quality matters more than speed to us. We appreciate the honesty.

d4e3_c_decline.speaker: Corporate Buyer
d4e3_c_decline.text: I understand. Perhaps we can revisit when your capacity grows. Here is my card.

d4e3_result_accept.speaker: Mentor
d4e3_result_accept.text: Big orders under pressure test your operations. Overcommitting can harm quality and burn out your team even if the revenue looks good.
